description:
At a minimum, the audit system should collect file deletion events for all users and root. If the auditd daemon is configured to use the augenrules program to read audit rules during daemon startup (the default), add the following line to a file with suffix .rules in the directory /etc/audit/rules.d, setting ARCH to either b32 for 32-bit system, or having two lines for both b32 and b64 in case your system is 64-bit: 
-a always,exit -F arch=ARCH -S renameat2 -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=delete
If the auditd daemon is configured to use the auditctl utility to read audit rules during daemon startup, add the following line to /etc/audit/audit.rules file, setting ARCH to either b32 for 32-bit system, or having two lines for both b32 and b64 in case your system is 64-bit: 
-a always,exit -F arch=ARCH -S renameat2 -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=delete
rationale:
Auditing file deletions will create an audit trail for files that are removed
from the system. The audit trail could aid in system troubleshooting, as well as, detecting
malicious processes that attempt to delete log files to conceal their presence.
ocil:
To determine if the system is configured to audit calls to the renameat2 system call, run the following command: 
$ sudo grep "renameat2" /etc/audit/audit.*
If the system is configured to audit this activity, it will return a line.
fixtext:
Configure the audit system to generate an audit event for any successful/unsuccessful use of the "renameat2" system call by adding or updating the following rules in "/etc/audit/audit.rules" and adding the following rules to "/etc/audit/rules.d/delete.rules" or updating the existing rules in files in the "/etc/audit/rules.d/" directory:


-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S renameat2 -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k delete
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S renameat2 -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k delete
It's allowed to group this system call within the same line as "rename", "unlink", "rmdir", "renameat", and "unlinkat".

The audit daemon must be restarted for the changes to take effect.
srg_requirement: Successful/unsuccessful uses of the renameat2 system call in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 must generate an audit record.

stig.srg_requirement: Successful/unsuccessful uses of the renameat2 system call in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 must generate an audit record.
stig.vuldiscussion:
Auditing file deletions will create an audit trail for files that are removed
from the system. The audit trail could aid in system troubleshooting, as well as, detecting
malicious processes that attempt to delete log files to conceal their presence.
stig.checktext:
To determine if the system is configured to audit calls to the
 renameat2 system call, run the following command:
 $ sudo grep "renameat2" /etc/audit/audit.*
If the system is configured to audit this activity, it will return a line.


If no line is returned, then this is a finding.
stig.fixtext:
Configure the audit system to generate an audit event for any successful/unsuccessful use of the "renameat2" system call by adding or updating the following rules in the "/etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules" file:
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S renameat2 -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k delete
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S renameat2 -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k delete

It's allowed to group this system call within the same line as "rename", "unlink", "rmdir", "renameat2", and "unlinkat".

The audit daemon must be restarted for the changes to take effect.

title: Ensure auditd Collects File Deletion Events by User - renameat2
